description:
Road rules in Queensland are laws that all road users must obey.

Drivers must follow traffic signs, traffic lights, road markings, and road rules at all times.

Police officers and authorised traffic controllers have the legal authority to direct traffic.

If a direction from a police officer or traffic controller conflicts with a sign or signal, the direction must be followed.

Failing to obey a lawful direction is an offence.

Drivers are legally responsible for driving with due care and attention at all times.

A driver must not drive in a careless, dangerous, or negligent manner.

Drivers must take reasonable steps to avoid crashes and reduce risk to others.

The driver is responsible for the safe operation of their vehicle under all conditions.

Giving way means slowing down and stopping if necessary to avoid a collision.

Drivers must give way when required by signs, road rules, or traffic conditions.

A driver entering a road from a driveway, car park, or footpath must give way to all vehicles and pedestrians.

Failing to give way when required is an offence.

At intersections, drivers must obey traffic lights, signs, and road markings.

If there are no signs or signals, drivers must give way according to Queensland road rules.

Drivers turning right must give way to oncoming traffic going straight or turning left.

Pedestrians must also be given priority where required.

When approaching a roundabout, drivers must give way to vehicles already in the roundabout.

Drivers must give way to vehicles approaching from the right.

Correct signalling is required when entering and exiting roundabouts.

Failing to give way at a roundabout can cause serious collisions.

Traffic lights control the movement of vehicles and pedestrians.

A red light means stop and remain stopped.

A yellow light means stop if it is safe to do so.

A green light allows you to proceed, but you must still give way where required.

U-turns are permitted in Queensland unless a sign prohibits them.

Drivers must ensure the U-turn can be completed safely.

A driver making a U-turn must give way to all other vehicles and pedestrians.

U-turns must not be made where visibility is limited or unsafe.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians on pedestrian crossings.

When turning at an intersection, drivers must give way to pedestrians crossing the road being entered.

Extra care is required near schools, shopping areas, and public transport stops.

Drivers must give way to emergency vehicles using flashing lights or sirens.

This may require slowing down, moving aside, or stopping if necessary.

Drivers must not obstruct the path of emergency vehicles or follow them closely.

Traffic signs provide legal instructions and warnings to road users.

Drivers must obey regulatory signs such as Stop, Give Way, and Speed Limit signs.

Warning signs alert drivers to potential hazards ahead.

Ignoring road signs is a breach of road law.

Regulatory signs give instructions that drivers must obey by law.

These include signs such as Stop, Give Way, No Entry, No Parking, and speed limit signs.

Failing to follow a regulatory sign is an offence.

Warning signs alert drivers to potential hazards or changes in road conditions ahead.

They are usually yellow and diamond-shaped.

Drivers should slow down and prepare to respond safely.

Speed limit signs show the maximum legal speed for a road or area.

Drivers must not exceed the posted speed limit.

Speed limits may change depending on conditions such as roadworks or school zones.

Traffic lights control traffic at intersections.

A red light means stop.

A yellow light means stop if it is safe to do so.

A green light means you may proceed if the intersection is clear.

Arrow traffic signals apply only to the direction shown by the arrow.

A green arrow allows movement in that direction only.

A red arrow means you must not proceed in that direction.

A Give Way sign requires drivers to slow down and give way to all conflicting traffic.

A Stop sign requires drivers to come to a complete stop and give way before proceeding.

Drivers must obey the sign even if the road appears clear.

Lane control signals are used above lanes to manage traffic flow.

A green arrow means the lane is open.

A red cross or white X means the lane must not be used.

Pedestrian signals indicate when pedestrians may cross the road.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians who are crossing when the pedestrian signal allows them to do so.

Special purpose signs include signs such as Shared Zone, Bus Lane, and School Zone signs.

These signs apply specific rules that drivers must follow in those areas.

Temporary signs are used for situations such as roadworks, crashes, or special events.

Drivers must obey temporary signs even if they differ from permanent signs.

Reduced speed limits often apply in roadwork areas.

In Queensland, the default speed limit in a built-up area is 50 km/h unless otherwise signed.

Drivers must always check for posted speed signs as limits may change.

The maximum speed limit in Queensland is 110 km/h unless a lower limit is signed.

Drivers must never exceed the posted speed limit.

School zone speed limits apply during the times shown on the signs.

Drivers must obey the signed school zone speed limit even if children are not visible.

Reduced speed limits apply in roadwork zones from the first speed reduction sign until an end sign.

Drivers must obey these limits even if workers are not present.

In good driving conditions, drivers should maintain a minimum following distance of at least two seconds.

This allows time to react and stop safely.
In wet or poor conditions, drivers should increase their following distance to allow for longer stopping distances.

Stopping distance includes reaction distance and braking distance.

As speed increases, total stopping distance increases significantly.

Wet, gravel, or slippery roads increase braking distance.

Drivers must slow down to suit road conditions.

In shared zones, the speed limit is 10 km/h.

Drivers must be prepared to stop for pedestrians at all times.

Drivers are always responsible for controlling their speed.

Speed warning systems or vehicle technology do not remove this responsibility.

Drivers must always drive with due care and attention.

If you feel too tired, unwell, or unfit to drive safely, you must stop driving and rest before continuing.
If you are involved in a crash causing injury or death, you must stop, assist if possible, and contact emergency services immediately.
Before driving, the driver is responsible for ensuring the vehicle is roadworthy and safe to operate.
If a crash causes property damage and the owner is not present, you must stop and leave your details or report the crash to police.
Drivers must produce their driver licence or provide their details when requested by a police officer.
A crash must be reported to police when there is injury, death, or significant property damage.
Drivers are responsible for ensuring that all passengers are correctly restrained with seatbelts or approved restraints.
After a minor crash with no injuries, drivers must stop and exchange details with the other party involved.
If your vision becomes impaired while driving, you must stop driving until it is safe to continue.
If another driver leaves the scene of a crash, you should report the incident to police as soon as possible.

In Queensland, the maximum blood alcohol concentration (BAC) for an open licence holder is 0.05.

Learner and provisional drivers must have a BAC of 0.00 at all times.

Alcohol affects driving ability from the first drink.

It slows reaction time, reduces coordination, and impairs judgment.

Driving with illegal drugs in your system is an offence in Queensland.

You can be charged even if you do not feel impaired.

Roadside drug tests commonly detect cannabis, MDMA, and methamphetamine.

Police can require a saliva test at any time.

Some prescription and over-the-counter medications can affect your ability to drive.

Always check warnings and avoid driving if unsure.

Defensive driving means anticipating potential hazards and the actions of other road users.

The goal is to reduce crash risk by planning ahead and maintaining safety margins.

A key principle of defensive driving is maintaining a safety buffer around your vehicle.

This space allows time to react if traffic conditions change suddenly.

If another driver behaves aggressively, the safest response is to create space and avoid engagement.

Do not respond with aggressive driving.

Planning ahead helps drivers avoid sudden braking or sharp steering.

Smooth, early decisions improve vehicle control and safety.

Hazard perception involves identifying potential dangers early and responding before they become critical.

Drivers should scan well ahead and to the sides, not just the vehicle in front.

Parked cars, pedestrians, cyclists, and intersections are common sources of hazards.

Drivers should be alert for sudden movements or changes in traffic flow.

Checking mirrors regularly helps drivers stay aware of surrounding traffic.

This awareness is essential for safe lane changes and braking.

Road conditions such as rain, gravel, or poor visibility require drivers to reduce speed.

Drivers must adjust their driving to suit the conditions.

Wet roads reduce tyre grip and increase stopping distance.

Drivers should slow down and increase following distance in wet conditions.

Reduced visibility due to fog, rain, or glare requires drivers to slow down and increase following distance.

Headlights should be used appropriately to improve visibility.

At an uncontrolled intersection, a driver must give way to vehicles approaching from the right.

This rule applies when there are no traffic lights, stop signs, or give way signs.

At a T-intersection, the driver on the terminating road must give way to vehicles on the continuing road.

When entering a road from a driveway or footpath, the driver must give way to all vehicles and pedestrians on the road.

When turning left at an intersection, drivers must give way to pedestrians crossing the road they are entering.

When turning right at an intersection, drivers must give way to oncoming vehicles travelling straight ahead or turning left.

When making a U-turn, a driver must give way to all other vehicles and pedestrians.

Before turning at an intersection, drivers must signal and check for traffic, cyclists, and pedestrians.

When entering a roundabout, drivers must give way to vehicles already in the roundabout.

Drivers must signal left when exiting a roundabout to inform other road users of their intention to leave.

Drivers should approach roundabouts at a speed that allows them to give way safely and stop if required.

A single continuous centre line means drivers must not cross or straddle the line.

Crossing is only allowed in very limited circumstances, such as entering or leaving a property if permitted.

A broken centre line indicates that drivers may cross the line when it is safe to do so.

This is commonly used for overtaking or turning.

A double continuous centre line means drivers must not cross or straddle the lines under any circumstances.

White edge lines mark the edge of the roadway and help drivers identify the trafficable area of the road.

Overtaking is prohibited whenever it is unsafe or when signs or road markings prohibit it.

Drivers must always assess visibility and oncoming traffic before overtaking.

The driver overtaking is fully responsible for ensuring the manoeuvre can be completed safely.

When overtaking a cyclist, drivers must allow sufficient lateral clearance to ensure the cyclist’s safety.

Before changing lanes, drivers must signal their intention and give way to vehicles already in the lane they are moving into.

A lane marked with a bicycle symbol indicates a bicycle lane reserved for cyclists.

Drivers must not drive in a bicycle lane unless permitted.

Bus lanes marked by signs and road markings may only be used by permitted vehicles.

Drivers must obey the operating times shown on signs.

Parking too close to a corner is unsafe because it blocks sight lines for drivers and pedestrians.

Parking on the wrong side of the road facing against traffic is unsafe and may be illegal in many situations.

Stopping or parking on a pedestrian crossing is prohibited because it endangers pedestrians and blocks visibility.

Before opening your door after parking near traffic, you must check mirrors and blind spots.

This helps prevent collisions with vehicles, cyclists, or other road users.

At a Stop sign, drivers must come to a complete stop at the stop line or before entering the intersection.

You should only reverse or adjust position at traffic lights if it is safe and does not endanger other road users.

At pedestrian crossings without traffic lights, drivers approaching the crossing must give way to pedestrians who are on or entering the crossing.

At traffic lights, drivers must give way to pedestrians who have a green walk signal.

When overtaking a cyclist, drivers must allow sufficient lateral clearance to ensure the cyclist’s safety.

Cyclists are legitimate road users and may ride on the road, in bicycle lanes, or on shared paths where permitted.
In shared zones, pedestrians have priority over vehicles and drivers must travel at very low speeds.

Elderly or disabled pedestrians may move slowly or have reduced awareness, requiring drivers to allow extra time and space.

When approaching pedestrians with a white cane or guide dog, drivers should slow down and be prepared to stop.

When driving in heavy rain, drivers should reduce speed and increase following distance.

Rain reduces visibility and tyre grip, increasing stopping distances.

Driving through floodwater is dangerous because the depth and condition of the road may be unknown.

Floodwater can wash vehicles away or hide road damage.

Fog significantly reduces visibility.

Drivers should slow down, use low beam headlights, and be prepared to stop.

Strong winds can affect vehicle stability, especially for high-sided vehicles.

Drivers should reduce speed and maintain firm steering control.

Aquaplaning occurs when tyres lose contact with the road surface due to water.

Drivers should ease off the pedals and steer smoothly if this occurs.

On rural roads, animals may enter the roadway unexpectedly.

Drivers should remain alert and be prepared to slow down or stop.

Night driving on rural roads is hazardous due to reduced visibility and increased wildlife activity.

If an animal suddenly appears on the road, drivers should brake firmly and steer safely without swerving dangerously.

When approaching roadworks, drivers must obey temporary signs and reduced speed limits.

These rules apply even if workers are not visible.

Temporary speed limits at roadworks exist to protect workers and road users.

Drivers must not exceed these limits.

When an emergency vehicle approaches using flashing lights or sirens, drivers must move out of the way safely and allow it to pass.

On multi-lane roads, drivers should indicate and move to a clear lane or to the left where possible to allow emergency vehicles to pass.

If stopped at traffic lights and an emergency vehicle approaches from behind, drivers may move carefully past the stop line if it is safe to do so.

Drivers must never block the path of an emergency vehicle, regardless of road type or traffic conditions.

If you cannot immediately move aside for an emergency vehicle, continue driving safely until you can pull over without creating danger.

When passing a stopped emergency vehicle with flashing lights, drivers should slow down and proceed with caution.

Drivers must obey directions given by police officers or authorised traffic controllers, even if they conflict with road signs or signals.

A police officer or authorised traffic controller has legal authority to direct traffic differently from signs or signals.

If police direct you to stop, turn, detour, or pull over, you must comply safely and as soon as possible.

Failing to follow a lawful police direction is an offence and may result in fines, demerit points, or licence suspension.

In Queensland, the driver is legally responsible for ensuring that all passengers under 16 years of age wear a seatbelt or approved restraint.

Seatbelts must be worn at all times when the vehicle is moving if a seatbelt is fitted.

A seatbelt should be worn firmly across the shoulder and low across the hips to reduce the risk of injury in a crash.

Seatbelts must not be worn incorrectly, such as under the arm or twisted, as this greatly increases the risk of serious injury.

Infants must be restrained in an approved rear-facing child restraint because it provides the best protection for the head and neck.

Children should only move from a booster seat to an adult seatbelt when they meet the minimum legal age and size requirements.

Child restraints must be correctly fitted and adjusted because incorrect fitting significantly reduces protection in a crash.

Unrestrained passengers can be seriously injured or cause injury to others in a crash due to uncontrolled movement.

If a passenger distracts the driver, the safest action is to ask them to stop so the driver can focus on driving safely.

Passengers should always check for traffic and cyclists before opening vehicle doors to prevent collisions and injuries.

Under-inflated tyres reduce handling and increase stopping distance.

They also increase heat buildup and can raise the risk of tyre failure.

Tyres should be checked regularly for tread depth and correct inflation pressure.

Good tread helps maintain grip, especially in wet conditions.

Headlights must work at night and whenever visibility is poor.

Working lights help you see hazards and help other road users see you.

Brake lights are important because they warn other drivers that you are slowing or stopping.

Faulty brake lights increase the risk of rear-end crashes.

The driver is responsible for ensuring a vehicle is roadworthy.

Regular checks and servicing help identify and fix safety issues early.

Loads must be secured so they cannot fall or move and create danger for other road users.

An insecure load can cause crashes, injuries, and damage.

If a load is placed too high or unevenly, vehicle stability can be reduced.

This increases the risk of rollover and loss of control.

Overloading a vehicle can reduce braking and handling performance.

It can also increase tyre and suspension stress, raising crash risk.

If a load protrudes from the rear of the vehicle, it must be secured and made visible if required.

Poorly secured protruding loads can strike other road users or fall onto the road.

Towing a trailer increases stopping distance because the vehicle has extra weight to slow down.

Drivers should allow more space and brake earlier.

Before towing, drivers should check the coupling, trailer lights, safety chain, and that the load is secure.

Towing with faults can lead to detachment or crashes.

If a trailer starts to sway, the safest response is to ease off the accelerator and keep steering steady.

Harsh braking or acceleration can worsen the sway.

Heavy vehicles need much longer to stop than cars.

Cutting in front of a truck removes its stopping space and can cause serious crashes.

Trucks have large blind spots close beside them and directly behind them.

Avoid driving in these areas because the driver may not see you.

Trucks may need extra space and can turn wide, especially near intersections.

It is unsafe to overtake a truck just before an intersection.
